title: 2 Kings 13
subtitle: Elisha’s Death — The Arrow of Victory

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Fall of Northern Kingdom

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — Jehoahaz; Israel Oppressed by Aram

panels.ctx: Jehoahaz (Jehu’s son) reigns 17 years and follows Jeroboam’s sins. Hazael and Ben-Hadad of Aram crush Israel until Jehoahaz’s army is reduced to 50 horsemen, 10 chariots, and 10,000 foot soldiers — a force barely sufficient for border defence. In desperation Jehoahaz “sought the LORD’s favour, and the LORD listened” — a rare moment of repentance in the northern kingdom. God sends a deliverer (unnamed), but the people still don’t remove the Asherah pole in Samaria.

cross.note: The “deliverer” pattern echoes Judges: Israel sins, is oppressed, cries out, God raises a deliverer. The cycle repeats even in the monarchy.
Hazael began reducing Israel during Jehu’s reign. The Aramean oppression spans two generations.

notes.note: God’s willingness to hear even a partially repentant king teaches that he does not wait for perfect contrition. He responds to the first movement of the heart toward him, however halting.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “deliverer” (mōshīaʿ) uses the same term as the judges-era saviours. The narrative deliberately links the monarchy’s crisis to the pre-monarchic pattern.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The army figures — 50 horsemen, 10 chariots, 10,000 infantry — represent a dramatic reduction from Solomon’s era (1 Kgs 10:26: 1,400 chariots, 12,000 horsemen). Assyrian records from Shalmaneser III’s era show Ahab fielding 2,000 chariots at the Battle of Qarqar. The decline is verifiable.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The narrator’s note that God “listened” to a king who continued in Jeroboam’s sins is theologically remarkable. It suggests that God’s compassion operates even when repentance is incomplete — a theme that challenges strict retribution theology.

sections.header: Verses 10–25 — Jehoash; Elisha’s Deathbed Prophecy; Elisha’s Bones

heb.paragraph: Elisha tells Jehoash to shoot an arrow eastward (v.17) and declares it “the arrow of the LORD’s victory, the arrow of victory over Aram.” The arrow is both a military weapon and a prophetic symbol — the direction (east, toward Aram) and the prophet’s declaration transform a physical act into a covenant promise.

panels.ctx: Jehoash (Joash) of Israel visits the dying Elisha and weeps: “My father! The chariots and horses of Israel!” — the same cry Elisha made for Elijah (2:12). Elisha tells him to shoot an arrow and then strike the ground with the remaining arrows. Jehoash strikes three times and stops. Elisha is angry: “You should have struck five or six times; then you would have destroyed Aram completely.” Partial obedience yields partial victory. Then Elisha dies and is buried. Later, a dead man thrown into Elisha’s tomb touches the prophet’s bones and revives — even in death, prophetic power persists.

cross.note: Elisha’s cry for Elijah — “My father! The chariots and horses of Israel!” — is now echoed by Jehoash for Elisha. The title transfers across prophetic generations.
At Jesus’ death, tombs opened and bodies were raised. Elisha’s bone-miracle anticipates the theme of death unable to contain God’s power.

notes.note: The miracle at Elisha’s grave teaches that God’s power is not extinguished by death. The same God who raised the Shunammite’s son through the living prophet now raises a stranger through the dead prophet’s bones. Death cannot limit omnipotence.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “arrow of victory” (ḥēts tĕshûʿah) combines military and theological language. The shooting of the arrow is a prophetic enacted sign — the physical action initiates the spiritual reality.

wiseman.source: Donald J. Wiseman, 1 and 2 Kings, Tyndale OT Commentary (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moabite raiding bands entering Israel in the spring were a regular feature of the period. The Moabite Stone confirms ongoing hostility between the two kingdoms throughout the 9th century.
The resurrection at the prophet’s tomb has no ANE parallel. Other cultures attributed power to the graves of holy men, but spontaneous resurrection through contact with bones is unique to this text.

provan.source: Iain W. Provan, 1 and 2 Kings, New International Biblical Commentary (1995) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The arrow episode teaches that prophetic promises are not self-executing; they require human participation at full intensity. Half-hearted response to God’s invitation yields half-hearted results.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVSo the Lord\u2019s anger burned against Israel, and for a long time he kept them under the power of Hazael king of Aram and Ben-Hadad his son.ESVAnd the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he gave them continually into the hand of Hazael king of Syria and into the hand of Ben-hadad the son of Hazael.NIVBut they did not turn away from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, which he had caused Israel to commit; they continued in them. Also, the Asherah pole remained standing in Samaria.ESVNevertheless, they did not depart from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, which he made Israel to sin, but walked in them; and the Asherah also remained in Samaria.)
